prompt: In the landmark Obermeyer et al. (2019) study, the Optum algorithm was biased because it used which proxy variable for healthcare need?

options: Healthcare costs
Number of diagnoses
Patient age
Insurance type

hints: The algorithm's purpose was to identify patients needing extra care.
Black patients historically incur lower costs at the same level of illness.
explanation: The Optum algorithm used healthcare costs as a proxy for healthcare needs. Because Black patients historically incur lower healthcare costs than white patients at the same level of illness — due to access barriers, distrust, and systemic inequities — the algorithm systematically under-identified Black patients for care management programs. This is the canonical example of proxy variable bias in healthcare AI.

prompt: What type of bias occurs when a training dataset does not proportionally represent all patient populations?

options: Representation bias
Historical bias
Measurement bias
Deployment bias

hints: It relates to who is included (or excluded) from the data.
Dermatology AI with <5% dark skin images is an example.
explanation: Representation bias occurs when training data does not proportionally represent all populations the model will be used on. The classic example is dermatology AI datasets containing fewer than 5% images of darker skin tones, leading to reduced accuracy for those patients. Historical bias reflects past inequities in the data; measurement bias involves biased clinical measurements; deployment bias involves using a model outside its intended context.

prompt: Pulse oximeters measure blood oxygen saturation equally accurately across all skin pigmentations.
hints: A landmark NEJM study during COVID-19 revealed a systematic bias.
Melanin affects light absorption at the wavelengths used by pulse oximeters.
explanation: False. Pulse oximeters systematically overestimate oxygen saturation in patients with darker skin pigmentation. A 2020 NEJM study found this bias led to occult hypoxemia — hidden low oxygen — in Black and Hispanic patients during COVID-19. The sensors were calibrated primarily on lighter-skinned individuals. This measurement bias affected clinical decisions about supplemental oxygen and contributed to worse outcomes.

prompt: Match each bias type with its mechanism.

pairs.right: Training data reflects past healthcare inequities
Clinical measurements themselves are systematically inaccurate for some groups
A single model applied across heterogeneous populations fails subgroups

right: The model was intentionally designed to discriminate

hints: Historical bias comes from the past, not the present.
Measurement bias is about the measurement tools, not the data or model.
explanation: Each bias type has a distinct mechanism: Historical bias reflects inequities embedded in past data (e.g., differential treatment patterns). Representation bias comes from demographic gaps in training data. Measurement bias originates in the clinical measurement tools themselves (e.g., pulse oximeters, spirometry references). Aggregation bias occurs when a model that works on average fails for specific subgroups within a heterogeneous population.

prompt: Categorize each scenario as an example of the specified bias pipeline stage.

items.text: Defining 'high-risk patient' as 'patient likely to incur high costs' rather than 'patient with unmet health needs'
Building a readmission model using only data from academic medical centers
Using ICD-10 codes as disease labels when diagnostic rates differ by race
Using a clinical NLP model trained on academic center notes at a community health center

hints: Problem formulation bias occurs before any data is collected.
Label bias affects the ground truth used for training.
explanation: Bias enters at every pipeline stage: Problem formulation bias occurs when the problem definition encodes inequity (cost as proxy for need). Data collection bias occurs when the sample is not representative. Label bias occurs when ground-truth labels reflect biased clinical practices. Deployment bias occurs when a model is used outside its intended context.

prompt: What does the fairness metric 'equalized odds' require?

options: Equal true positive rates AND equal false positive rates across demographic groups
Equal prediction rates across demographic groups regardless of actual disease rates
Equal positive predictive values across demographic groups
Equal AUROC values across demographic groups

hints: Equalized odds accounts for differences in base rates between groups.
It ensures the model catches the same proportion of positive cases in each group.
explanation: Equalized odds requires that both the true positive rate (sensitivity) and false positive rate be equal across demographic groups. This means the model catches the same proportion of actual cases in each group (equal sensitivity) and generates the same proportion of false alarms in each group (equal FPR). It accounts for different base rates between groups, unlike demographic parity which ignores them.

prompt: When disease prevalence differs between demographic groups, it is mathematically possible to simultaneously achieve demographic parity, equalized odds, and predictive parity.
hints: This is related to the impossibility theorem in algorithmic fairness.
Chouldechova (2017) and Kleinberg et al. (2016) proved this result.
explanation: False. The impossibility theorem (Chouldechova, 2017; Kleinberg et al., 2016) proves that when base rates differ between groups, it is mathematically impossible to simultaneously satisfy all three fairness metrics. This means fairness in healthcare AI requires a value judgment about which metric to prioritize — there is no purely technical solution. The choice depends on the clinical context and the relative costs of different types of errors.

prompt: For a cancer screening AI, which fairness metric should be prioritized?

options: Equalized odds — ensuring equal sensitivity so no demographic group has more missed cancers
Demographic parity — ensuring equal screening rates regardless of cancer prevalence
Predictive parity — ensuring equal PPV across groups
No fairness metric is needed for screening programs

hints: Missing a cancer case is far more harmful than a false positive.
Screening programs should catch cancer equally well across all groups.
explanation: For cancer screening, equalized odds (especially equal sensitivity) should be prioritized because missing a cancer case in any demographic group is unacceptable. Unequal sensitivity means some populations have more missed cancers, leading to delayed diagnosis and worse outcomes. Demographic parity is less appropriate here because cancer prevalence may genuinely differ between groups.

prompt: Match each bias detection tool with its developer.
hints: AIF360 is one of the most comprehensive open-source fairness toolkits.
Fairlearn focuses on assessment and constrained optimization.
explanation: AI Fairness 360 (IBM) provides 70+ fairness metrics and 10+ mitigation algorithms. Fairlearn (Microsoft) focuses on assessment and constrained optimization. Aequitas (University of Chicago) is designed for auditing ML in policy contexts. Google's What-If Tool provides interactive exploration of model behavior across subgroups. All are open-source and widely used in healthcare AI bias evaluation.

prompt: Arrange the steps of a structured bias audit in the correct order.

items: Benchmark against existing standard of care
correctOrder: Benchmark against existing standard of care

hints: You must define what groups to evaluate before computing metrics.
Documentation should be the final step after all analyses are complete.
explanation: A structured bias audit follows a logical sequence: first define the protected attributes (race, sex, age, etc.), then compute performance metrics disaggregated by each group, perform intersectional analysis (combinations of attributes), analyze whether the model relies on sensitive features (SHAP/LIME), compare fairness to the current standard of care, and finally document all findings in a comprehensive audit report.

prompt: The eGFR formula historically included a race-based ___ factor that made kidney disease appear less severe in Black patients.
hints: It was a mathematical adjustment applied based on the patient's reported race.
It was removed in 2021 following advocacy by the NKF and ASN.
explanation: The CKD-EPI eGFR equation included a race correction (multiplier) that assigned higher eGFR values to Black patients, making their kidney disease appear less severe. This delayed referral to nephrology and transplant evaluation. In 2021, a joint NKF-ASN task force recommended removing the race variable and using a cystatin C-based equation instead, recognizing that the race correction encoded social factors as biological race differences.

prompt: An AI model has an overall AUROC of 0.85. When stratified by race, it shows AUROC of 0.90 for white patients and 0.65 for Black patients. What does this reveal?

options: The aggregate metric hides a significant performance disparity — the model works well for one group but poorly for another
The model is well-calibrated
The model is performing acceptably for both groups
External validation is needed before any conclusions can be drawn

hints: 0.65 AUROC is near the threshold of clinically useless.
Aggregate metrics can mask subgroup-level disparities.
explanation: This is a textbook example of aggregate metrics hiding disparities. The overall AUROC of 0.85 looks acceptable, but it conceals a 25-point gap between groups. An AUROC of 0.65 for Black patients is barely better than chance (0.5) and clinically inadequate. This is why FDA guidance and responsible AI frameworks require disaggregated performance reporting by demographic subgroups.

prompt: Algorithmic bias in healthcare AI is always the result of intentional discrimination by developers.
hints: Think about how training data inherits systemic inequities.
Most bias arises from data and design choices, not malicious intent.
explanation: False. Most algorithmic bias in healthcare arises from well-intentioned teams using data that reflects systemic inequities already embedded in the healthcare system. Historical bias (past unequal treatment patterns), representation bias (who is in the data), and measurement bias (biased clinical instruments) are all unintentional but harmful. Recognizing that bias is structural, not just intentional, is the first step toward systematic mitigation.

prompt: A deployed risk prediction model has lower sensitivity for elderly women compared to other demographic groups. Which post-processing intervention could most directly address this disparity?

options: Use a lower classification threshold for elderly women to equalize sensitivity across groups
Remove age and sex from the model entirely
Retrain the model from scratch using only elderly women's data
Display a disclaimer that the model is less accurate for elderly women

hints: Post-processing interventions adjust the model's outputs, not its training.
A lower threshold means the model flags more patients in that group.
explanation: Group-specific threshold adjustment is the most direct post-processing intervention for equalizing sensitivity. By lowering the classification threshold for elderly women, more cases in that group will be flagged, increasing sensitivity to match other groups. This is commonly used in practice because it can be applied without retraining the model. Removing age and sex would hide the disparity without fixing it. Retraining on only one group would reduce overall performance.

prompt: Categorize each bias mitigation technique as pre-processing, in-processing, or post-processing.

items.text: Reweighting training data to give higher weight to underrepresented groups
Adding a fairness constraint to the model's optimization objective
Using group-specific classification thresholds to equalize sensitivity
SMOTE oversampling of minority group examples
Routing uncertain predictions to human review

hints: Pre-processing changes the data before training.
In-processing changes the training algorithm itself.
explanation: Pre-processing techniques modify training data (reweighting, resampling with SMOTE). In-processing techniques modify the training algorithm (fairness constraints, adversarial debiasing). Post-processing techniques modify model outputs after prediction (threshold adjustment, reject option classification). Comprehensive bias mitigation typically combines interventions at all three stages.

prompt: Read the scenario and answer the questions about bias detection and mitigation.

config.scenario: Your health system has deployed a heart failure readmission prediction model. After 6 months of monitoring, you discover that the model's sensitivity is 85% for white patients but only 62% for Black patients. The model uses structured EHR data including prior healthcare utilization, medication adherence scores, and social determinants proxied by ZIP code.

questions.prompt: Which feature is most likely contributing to the racial disparity in model performance?
options: Prior healthcare utilization — it encodes access barriers that disproportionately affect Black patients
The model's neural network architecture
The EHR system's database structure
The AUROC metric being used for evaluation

questions.prompt: What is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Apply a lower classification threshold for Black patients to equalize sensitivity while investigating root causes
Remove all race-related data from the model immediately
Shut down the model entirely
Wait for more data before taking action

hints: Healthcare utilization reflects access, not just clinical severity.
Post-processing threshold adjustment can be applied quickly while deeper investigation continues.
explanation: Prior healthcare utilization (ED visits, hospitalizations, outpatient visits) encodes access barriers that disproportionately affect Black patients — similar to the Optum cost-as-proxy-for-need problem. ZIP code proxies for social determinants also correlate with race. The most appropriate immediate action is threshold adjustment to equalize sensitivity (a quick post-processing fix) while conducting a deeper investigation into feature contributions and developing longer-term pre-processing or in-processing mitigations.

prompt: Removing race as a variable from a clinical AI model guarantees the model will not discriminate based on race.
hints: Think about proxy variables that correlate with race.
ZIP code, insurance type, and healthcare utilization all correlate with race.
explanation: False. Removing race from the model does not eliminate racial bias because many other variables serve as proxies for race: ZIP code encodes neighborhood-level racial composition, insurance type correlates with employment and income, healthcare utilization reflects access barriers, and language preference may correlate with race/ethnicity. The model can learn racial patterns through these proxies even without explicit race data. This is why fairness evaluation must examine performance across racial groups regardless of whether race is a model input.

prompt: What is the purpose of a Model Card (Mitchell et al., 2019) in the context of clinical AI accountability?

options: A standardized document describing model purpose, training data demographics, performance by subgroup, limitations, and ethical considerations
A credit card used to pay for cloud computing resources for model training
A visual representation of the model's neural network architecture
An internal developer reference for model hyperparameters

hints: It accompanies deployed models as a transparency document.
It includes disaggregated performance metrics.
explanation: A Model Card is a standardized documentation framework that accompanies deployed AI models. It describes the model's purpose, intended use population, training data demographics and sources, performance metrics disaggregated by demographic subgroups, known limitations and failure modes, and ethical considerations including fairness evaluation results. Model Cards are becoming a standard accountability mechanism for deployed clinical AI systems.

prompt: The fairness metric that requires the model's positive prediction rate to be equal across all demographic groups is called ___.
hints: It does not account for differences in actual disease prevalence between groups.
It focuses on equal prediction rates, not equal accuracy.
explanation: Demographic parity (also called statistical parity) requires that the proportion of positive predictions be equal across all demographic groups. If 10% of white patients are flagged as high-risk, 10% of Black patients should be too. Its main limitation is that if actual disease prevalence differs between groups, equal prediction rates may mean different accuracy levels for different groups.

prompt: Arrange the stages of community engagement for equitable health AI from earliest to latest in the development lifecycle.
hints: Community input should start before development begins.
Post-deployment monitoring is the final ongoing stage.
explanation: Effective community engagement spans the entire AI lifecycle: co-designing the problem definition with community members ensures the right problem is being solved, reviewing datasets for representation gaps catches bias early, prototype testing with diverse users identifies usability and fairness issues before deployment, pre-deployment impact review assesses potential community consequences, and post-deployment feedback collection enables ongoing monitoring and adjustment.

prompt: What is a 'feedback loop' in the context of algorithmic bias in healthcare?

options: A cycle where a biased model under-serves a group, generating less positive data for that group, which reinforces the bias in future retraining
A mechanism where clinicians provide feedback to improve the model
A software testing process where outputs are fed back as inputs
A patient satisfaction survey used to evaluate AI tools

hints: It is a self-reinforcing cycle that amplifies existing bias over time.
If a model under-predicts risk for a group, that group receives fewer interventions.
explanation: A bias feedback loop occurs when a biased model under-predicts risk for a group, leading to fewer interventions for that group. Fewer interventions mean fewer positive outcome data points. When the model is retrained, it learns even lower risk for that group, further reducing interventions — a self-reinforcing cycle. This is one of the most dangerous aspects of deployed biased AI because the bias compounds over time.

prompt: You are asked to conduct a bias audit of a sepsis prediction model deployed at your health system. Describe the specific protected attributes you would evaluate, the metrics you would compute for each subgroup, and what actions you would take if you found significant disparities.

config.rubric: Award full marks for: (1) identifying specific protected attributes (race/ethnicity, sex/gender, age groups, insurance type, primary language), (2) naming specific metrics (sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, calibration, AUROC for each subgroup, plus fairness metrics like equalized odds), (3) describing intersectional analysis, (4) outlining concrete actions for detected disparities (threshold adjustment, feature investigation, retraining, governance escalation).
config.sampleAnswer: I would evaluate the following protected attributes: race/ethnicity (White, Black, Hispanic, Asian, Other), sex/gender, age groups (18-44, 45-64, 65+), insurance type (commercial, Medicare, Medicaid, uninsured), and primary language (English vs non-English). For each subgroup, I would compute: AUROC, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, and calibration curves. I would also calculate equalized odds (comparing sensitivity and FPR across groups) and predictive parity (comparing PPV). Intersectional analysis would examine combinations like elderly Black women. If significant disparities were found, I would: (1) immediately apply group-specific threshold adjustment to equalize sensitivity as a short-term fix, (2) investigate feature contributions using SHAP to identify proxy variables driving the disparity, (3) explore pre-processing mitigations (reweighting, resampling), and (4) escalate to the AI governance committee with a documented bias audit report and recommended corrective actions.

hints: Think systematically: attributes, metrics, intersections, and actions.
Include both quantitative analysis and organizational response.
explanation: A comprehensive bias audit requires evaluating multiple protected attributes with disaggregated metrics, conducting intersectional analysis, investigating feature-level contributions to disparities, and having a clear action plan for detected bias. The combination of immediate post-processing fixes and longer-term root cause analysis is the standard approach.

prompt: SHAP values can reveal whether a clinical AI model is relying on protected attributes or their proxies in its predictions.
hints: SHAP shows each feature's contribution to a specific prediction.
If ZIP code has high SHAP importance and correlates with race, this raises a red flag.
explanation: True. SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values show each feature's contribution to individual predictions. If SHAP analysis reveals that ZIP code (a proxy for race and income) is a dominant predictor, this warrants investigation for proxy bias. While SHAP was not designed specifically as a fairness tool, it is widely used in bias audits to identify whether models are relying on features that encode protected attributes.

prompt: The bias mitigation technique where a second neural network tries to predict the protected attribute from the model's predictions — penalizing the main model when it succeeds — is called ___.
hints: It uses an adversary — a competing network.
The main model is trained to make predictions that the adversary cannot use to infer the protected attribute.
explanation: Adversarial debiasing is an in-processing technique where a main model and an adversary are trained simultaneously. The adversary tries to predict the protected attribute (e.g., race) from the main model's predictions. The main model is penalized when the adversary succeeds, forcing it to learn representations that are decorrelated from the protected attribute. This is implemented in IBM's AI Fairness 360 toolkit.

prompt: The VBAC (vaginal birth after cesarean) calculator was revised in 2021 to remove race/ethnicity variables. Why was the inclusion of race problematic?

options: It gave Black and Hispanic patients lower predicted success rates, potentially leading to more cesarean sections in these groups
Race data was frequently missing, making the calculator unreliable
Including race made the calculator too complex for clinical use
Race was included by accident during the development process

hints: Lower predicted success might influence counseling toward cesarean section.
Consider the downstream clinical impact of race-adjusted predictions.
explanation: The VBAC calculator included race/ethnicity as a predictor variable, which produced lower predicted success rates for Black and Hispanic patients. This influenced clinical counseling and may have led to higher rates of cesarean sections in these populations — a more invasive procedure with greater risk. The race variable encoded historical patterns of differential treatment rather than genuine biological differences in birth outcomes.

prompt: Read the scenario and answer the questions about accountability frameworks.

config.scenario: A health system is establishing an AI ethics committee to review all clinical AI deployments. The committee needs to create a tiered review process, define documentation requirements, and establish ongoing monitoring protocols.

questions.prompt: Which type of AI deployment should receive the MOST intensive review?
options: A diagnostic AI that autonomously determines treatment without clinician review
An administrative AI that optimizes operating room scheduling
A documentation tool that suggests ICD-10 codes for clinician approval
A patient portal chatbot that answers FAQ questions

questions.prompt: What documentation standard should accompany every deployed clinical AI model?
options: A Model Card describing purpose, training data demographics, disaggregated performance, and limitations
A one-page marketing summary from the vendor
Only the FDA clearance letter
A technical specification of the server hardware

hints: Higher autonomy and higher clinical stakes require more intensive review.
Model Cards are the standard documentation framework for deployed AI.
explanation: Autonomous clinical AI (making decisions without clinician review) requires the most intensive governance review due to the highest risk level — there is no human safety net. Documentation should follow the Model Card standard (Mitchell et al., 2019), which describes model purpose, training data demographics, disaggregated performance by subgroup, known limitations, and ethical considerations. This provides transparency for ongoing monitoring and accountability.

prompt: Explain the impossibility theorem in algorithmic fairness and describe its practical implications for healthcare AI. Why does this mean fairness requires value judgments rather than purely technical solutions?

config.rubric: Award full marks for: (1) correctly stating that demographic parity, equalized odds, and predictive parity cannot all be satisfied simultaneously when base rates differ, (2) explaining that disease prevalence often differs across demographics in healthcare, (3) articulating that the choice of which metric to prioritize depends on clinical context and value judgments about the relative costs of different types of errors.
config.sampleAnswer: The impossibility theorem (Chouldechova 2017, Kleinberg et al. 2016) proves that when the base rate of a condition differs between demographic groups — which is common in healthcare — it is mathematically impossible to simultaneously achieve demographic parity, equalized odds, and predictive parity. For example, if diabetes prevalence is 15% in one group and 8% in another, equal prediction rates (demographic parity) will produce unequal PPV (predictive parity mismatch). This means healthcare AI teams must choose which fairness metric to prioritize based on clinical context. For cancer screening, equal sensitivity (catching every case regardless of demographics) might be most important. For resource allocation, equal access might matter most. This choice is inherently a value judgment — it involves deciding whose errors are most costly and how to balance competing notions of fairness.

hints: The theorem was proven independently by two research groups.
Consider why different clinical contexts might prioritize different fairness metrics.
explanation: The impossibility theorem establishes that technical fairness solutions are inherently limited — you cannot satisfy all fairness definitions simultaneously when base rates differ. In healthcare, this means multidisciplinary teams (clinicians, ethicists, community representatives) must make explicit value judgments about which fairness metric best serves the specific clinical and population context.

prompt: Arrange these inter-rater agreement (Cohen's kappa) values from weakest to strongest agreement.
hints: Kappa > 0.8 indicates almost perfect agreement.
Kappa < 0.4 suggests the annotation task may be too subjective.
explanation: Cohen's kappa ranges from 0 (no agreement beyond chance) to 1 (perfect agreement). In clinical annotation: 0.35 indicates poor agreement (task may be too subjective), 0.45 is moderate, 0.55 is moderate to substantial, 0.72 is substantial, and 0.88 indicates almost perfect agreement. When training labels have low agreement, the model's performance ceiling is inherently limited.

prompt: Match each accountability component with its description.

pairs.right: Standardized documentation describing model purpose, performance by subgroup, and limitations
Documentation describing dataset motivation, composition, collection process, and intended uses
Multidisciplinary body that reviews, approves, and monitors AI deployments
Real-time tracking of model performance across demographic groups

right: An automated system that removes all biased predictions

hints: Model Cards focus on the model; Datasheets focus on the training data.
Governance committees are organizational structures, not tools.
explanation: Each accountability component serves a different function: Model Cards (Mitchell et al., 2019) document the model itself. Datasheets for Datasets (Gebru et al., 2021) document the training data. AI governance committees provide organizational oversight. Fairness dashboards enable real-time performance monitoring across demographic groups. Together, they form a comprehensive accountability framework.

prompt: A health system's AI governance committee is evaluating whether to deploy an imaging AI that shows equalized odds across racial groups but has a 3-percentage-point AUROC advantage over a simpler model that has never been tested for fairness. What should the committee recommend?

options: Deploy the fairness-tested model while requiring the vendor of the other model to provide disaggregated performance data before consideration
Deploy the higher-AUROC model because performance is the only consideration
Deploy neither model until both achieve perfect fairness
Deploy both models simultaneously and let clinicians choose

hints: A model without fairness testing is an unknown risk.
A 3-percentage-point AUROC difference may be clinically insignificant.
explanation: The governance committee should prefer the model with demonstrated fairness (equalized odds testing) over an untested model with slightly higher AUROC. A 3-point AUROC difference is often clinically insignificant, while undetected bias could cause systematic harm to specific patient populations. The untested model should not be considered until the vendor provides disaggregated performance data. This reflects the principle that demonstrated fairness is a deployment prerequisite, not an optional bonus.
